feedback.text: I am writing to you as a concerned European citizen to express my support for the Digital Fairness Act and to highlight the inclusion of an important area that needs further protection: Video games that are permanently deactivated by publishers after purchase. The current discussions on the Digital Fairness Act, addressing issues such as dark patterns, addiction and deceptive practices, are crucial. However, there is a growing and worrying trend where publishers stop accessing games that consumers have already bought. This not only affects the value of our investments, but also undermines the fundamental concept of ownership in the digital age. In recent years, hundreds of games have already been taken off the network by their publishers, resulting in players losing access to their purchased content. Many other games are currently at risk, especially those that rely on online servers without clear plans for their long-term preservation. This practice is unacceptable and contrary to the principles of fairness that the Digital Fairness Act seeks to promote. I urge you to extend the Digital Fairness Act to include provisions that protect consumers against the destruction of already sold video games by publishers. This could be done through rules requiring the availability of offline versions or the transfer of rights to third parties in case of server shutdown. Such a measure would not only strengthen consumer rights, but would also foster trust in the digital market as a whole. Millions of European citizens have expressed their support for the protection of video games, including through initiatives such as the European Citizens’ Initiative. This underlines the urgency and importance of this issue for a large number of voters. Thank you for your time and commitment to creating a fairer digital environment. I hope that my comments will be taken into account in your reflections. Yours sincerely, Robert Wenzel
